g.data-narration: This topic explains why cloud computing faces challenges in achieving green computing goals despite its initial promise of environmental benefits.
g.text: Challenges in Cloud Computing as Green Computing

g.data-narration: Data centers consume 1-2% of global electricity. Most servers run at only 15-30% utilization, wasting massive energy even when idle.
g.text: 1. High Energy Consumption in Data Centers
• Global data center energy: 1-2% of total electricity
• Server utilization: Only 15-30% on average
• Continuous operation leads to significant waste

g.data-narration: PUE measures data center efficiency. Most facilities have PUE 1.1-2.0, meaning they use 10-100% more energy than actual computing requires.
g.text: 2. Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) Challenge
PUE = Total Facility Energy ÷ IT Equipment Energy
• Ideal PUE: 1.0 | Typical range: 1.1-2.0 | Overhead: Cooling, lighting, power losses

g.data-narration: Virtualization enables server consolidation but adds hypervisor overhead. Live VM migration temporarily doubles resource usage, which directly contradicts green computing goals.
g.text: 3. Virtualization Limitations
• Hypervisor layer consumes CPU and memory resources
• Live migration: Both source and destination servers active
• Consolidation vs. performance trade-off complicates energy savings

g.data-narration: Cloud infrastructure generates significant e-waste through frequent server replacements every 3-5 years. Manufacturing requires rare earth minerals and highly energy-intensive processes.
g.text: 4. Electronic Waste Generation
• Server lifespan: 3-5 years due to continuous operation
• Manufacturing consumes rare earth minerals and high energy
• E-waste disposal challenges, especially in developing nations

g.data-narration: Cooling systems consume 30-50% of data center energy. Geographic location affects carbon footprint based on the local grid's renewable energy mix and efficiency.
g.text: 5. Carbon Footprint and Cooling Challenges
• Cooling systems: 30-50% of non-IT energy overhead
• Carbon footprint depends on data center location and energy grid
• Renewable energy adoption faces economic and technical constraints

g.data-narration: Multi-tenant clouds face resource allocation challenges. Tenants over-provision for peak loads, causing idle capacity. Lack of standardized green policies complicates sustainability.
g.text: 6. Multi-Tenancy and Policy Challenges
• Over-provisioning leads to idle capacity consuming energy
• No universal green computing standards across jurisdictions
• Inconsistent carbon reporting hinders informed decisions
